chunk_summary: This section outlines the importance of discharge phenotyping for preterm/LBW/ELBW infants to create tailored post-discharge nutrition plans, focusing on growth, disease, feeding, and medication phenotypes to optimize outcomes.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of discharge phenotyping in post-discharge nutrition planning for preterm/LBW/ELBW infants.
Identify key phenotype elements such as growth, disease, feeding, and medication profiles for individualized care.
Learn how to document and operationalize phenotype data in discharge summaries and clinic notes.
key_facts: Discharge phenotyping includes birth phenotype (e.g., GA, LBW, VLBW, ELBW, SGA, IUGR).
Growth phenotype includes weight, length, and head circumference z-scores, and EUGR or centile crossing.
Disease phenotype includes chronic respiratory morbidity, BPD, CHD, post-NEC, cholestasis, and neurodisability risk.
Feeding phenotype assesses breastfeeding type, transfer adequacy, and caloric density of formula.
Medication phenotype (e.g., diuretics, corticosteroids) impacts electrolyte, bone-mineral risk, and energy strategies.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Transitioning the breastfeeding preterm infant from the NICU to home requires careful planning to ensure adequate nutrition and growth support. (Academy of Breastfeeding Medicine, Clinical Protocol #12)
